Category: --- PROCUREMENT LIFECYCLE: the process of scoping, soliciting, evaluating, and awarding construction work ---

Description: Vetting of a contractor or vendor's financial health, safety record, bonding capacity, and relevant experience BEFORE they are invited to bid. The gatekeeping step that determines who is eligible to compete for work. Evaluates EMR, TRIR, financials, references, and license status.
Industry Standard: AGC Prequalification Standards; AIA A305 (Contractor's Qualification Statement); Dun & Bradstreet
Common Values: Financial review, safety record (EMR < 1.0), bonding capacity, trade license verification, project references
Source Systems: Procore (Prequalification tool); TradeTapp; ISNetworld; Avetta; BuildingConnected
Procore Coverage: None — Procore has a Prequalification tool but no tables in the data warehouse gold layer
Benchmarking Use: Prequalification pass rates and common disqualification reasons benchmark subcontractor pool quality by market
Notes: Procore Prequalification exists as a product but data is not yet in the analytics layer. TradeTapp and ISNetworld are major third-party platforms.

Description: A defined scope of work issued to qualified contractors for competitive pricing. Bundles drawings, specifications, schedule, and commercial terms into a single solicitation. The unit of procurement — one package per trade or work scope.
Industry Standard: AIA A701 (Instructions to Bidders); AGC; ConsensusDocs
Common Values: By trade: Concrete, Structural Steel, MEP, Drywall, Roofing, Elevators
Source Systems: Procore (Bidding tool); BuildingConnected; SmartBid; iSqFt; PlanHub
Procore Coverage: None — Procore has a Bidding module but no tables in the data warehouse gold layer
Benchmarking Use: Bid package count and coverage percentage reveal how much of the project scope is competitively bid vs. sole-sourced
Notes: Bid packages are typically organized by CSI MasterFormat division. The number of packages varies by delivery method (hard bid = many, CM/GC = fewer, design-build = fewest).

Description: Formal solicitation to assess contractor capabilities before inviting bids. Used on complex or specialized scopes where not all contractors can perform the work. Shortlists the field before pricing begins.
Industry Standard: AIA; AGC; federal FAR (for public work)
Common Values: RFQ for structural steel erection, curtain wall, elevator, fire protection
Procore Coverage: None — no RFQ-specific data in the data warehouse
Benchmarking Use: RFQ response rates by trade and market reveal subcontractor availability and competitive landscape
Notes: RFQs are most common on owner-driven projects and large GC programs. Design-build uses RFQs at the prime contract level.

Description: Detailed solicitation requesting scope, approach, schedule, and price from qualified contractors. More comprehensive than a simple bid — evaluates methodology, not just price. Common on negotiated or best-value procurements.
Common Values: Design-build RFP, CM-at-risk RFP, specialty trade RFP
Procore Coverage: None — no RFP-specific data in the data warehouse
Benchmarking Use: RFP-to-award conversion rates and response timelines benchmark procurement process efficiency
Notes: RFPs are the standard for design-build, CM/GC, and public best-value projects. Pure hard-bid projects skip RFPs and go straight to ITB.

Description: A contractor's formal response to an ITB, RFQ, or RFP — includes pricing, scope clarifications, exclusions, alternates, and schedule. The document that becomes the basis for contract negotiation upon award.
Common Values: Lump sum bid, unit price bid, GMP proposal, T&M proposal, design-build proposal
Procore Coverage: None — Procore Bidding module captures bids but data not in warehouse
Benchmarking Use: Bid spread (high-to-low ratio) and number of bidders per package benchmark market competitiveness
Notes: Bid leveling (comparing proposals across bidders) is typically done in Excel. Number of bidders per package is a key market health indicator.

Description: Comparative analysis of competing proposals — normalizing scope, pricing, qualifications, and exclusions to make an apples-to-apples recommendation. Identifies the apparent low bidder or best-value selection.
Common Values: Price comparison, scope gap analysis, exclusion identification, reference checks, schedule evaluation
Procore Coverage: None — bid leveling is manual/Excel-based even in Procore's Bidding module
Benchmarking Use: Average number of bidders per package, bid spread, and evaluation cycle time benchmark procurement competitiveness
Notes: Bid leveling is one of the most Excel-dependent processes in construction. No PM system fully automates scope normalization.

Description: Formal notification to the selected contractor that they have won the work and are authorized to begin. Triggers mobilization, procurement, and insurance/bonding requirements. May precede the executed contract via a Letter of Intent.
Common Values: Award letter, Letter of Intent (LOI), Notice to Proceed (NTP), verbal authorization
Procore Coverage: Low — award is implicit when a commitment is created in Procore, but no formal award/NTP tracking exists
Benchmarking Use: Time from bid opening to award/NTP benchmarks procurement decision speed and organizational agility
Notes: LOI vs. NTP vs. executed contract: many subs start work on an LOI before the contract is signed. This creates risk exposure for the GC.

Category: --- CONTRACT INSTRUMENTS: the executed agreements that define scope, price, and terms (financial detail in Financial Instruments domain) ---

Description: Executed agreement between a general contractor and a subcontractor for a defined scope of work at an agreed price. The primary commercial vehicle for trade work. Includes scope, price, schedule, insurance, and flow-down terms.
Common Values: Lump sum, GMP, unit price, T&M, cost-plus
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; Foundation; Spectrum
Procore Coverage: High — commitments table with type, status, value, vendor. Widely adopted.
Benchmarking Use: Contract value distribution by trade reveals where project dollars concentrate — drives negotiation leverage analysis
Notes: In Procore, subcontracts are stored as commitments with type classification. Financial detail (line items, COs, invoicing) lives in Financial Instruments domain.

Description: Agreement for material supply, equipment rental, or services at a specified price. Simpler than a subcontract — typically no labor/scope-of-work component. Tracks what was ordered, from whom, at what cost.
Common Values: Material PO, equipment rental PO, service PO
Procore Coverage: High — commitments table includes POs. materials_purchase_order table exists but NOT ready.
Benchmarking Use: PO volume and value by material category reveals procurement concentration and supply chain risk
Notes: In Procore, POs are commitments with a different type classification. The workforce.materials_purchase_order table would add detailed PO data when ready.

Description: Agreement between the project owner and the general contractor or CM. The top-level commercial agreement that defines the overall project price, scope, schedule, and terms. All subcontracts flow down from this.
Common Values: Lump sum, GMP, cost-plus, design-build, CM-at-risk, IPD
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Oracle Unifier
Procore Coverage: Low — prime_contracts table exists but is NOT ready for import in the data warehouse
Benchmarking Use: Prime contract type and value distribution benchmarks delivery method trends by market and project type
Notes: Prime contract data in Procore is sparse. Most GCs track prime contract terms in their ERP or contract management system, not Procore.

Description: Standing agreement with a vendor or subcontractor that covers multiple projects or a defined time period. Sets standard terms so individual project work orders can be issued without full contract negotiation each time.
Common Values: Annual MSA, multi-project MSA, vendor blanket PO
Procore Coverage: None — no MSA tracking in Procore. Each project commitment is standalone.
Benchmarking Use: MSA coverage rate (% of contracts under MSAs) benchmarks procurement maturity and vendor relationship management
Notes: MSAs are an enterprise procurement tool — most common on large programs or with repeat subcontractors. Procore's project-centric model doesn't support cross-project agreements.

Category: --- CONTRACT TERMS & EXHIBITS: the commercial provisions that define obligations, risks, and payment structure ---

Description: Detailed description of contracted work — defines what the subcontractor or vendor is responsible for delivering. May reference drawings, specifications, and schedule. The single most important exhibit to any construction contract.
Common Values: Division-based scope (03 Concrete, 09 Finishes), activity-based scope, area-based scope
Procore Coverage: Low — commitment description field exists but scope detail lives in attached documents, not structured data
Benchmarking Use: Scope completeness and clarity correlate with change order rates — vague scopes generate more changes
Notes: Scope of work is almost always a PDF/Word attachment, not structured data. NLP on commitment descriptions is the best path to scope classification.

Description: The standard legal and administrative terms that apply to all contracts on a project — defines roles, responsibilities, dispute resolution, insurance requirements, and procedural obligations. The 'rules of the game' for the project.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (General Conditions); AGC ConsensusDocs; EJCDC
Common Values: AIA A201, ConsensusDocs 200, modified/supplementary conditions, owner-specific
Source Systems: Legal document — not in PM software
Procore Coverage: None — general conditions are legal documents stored outside PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Modification frequency and type of supplementary conditions reveal risk allocation trends by owner type
Notes: GCs and owners negotiate supplementary conditions that modify the standard AIA or AGC terms. These modifications define the actual risk allocation.

Description: Contractual provision specifying the percentage of each payment withheld until substantial completion or final acceptance. Protects the owner/GC against incomplete or defective work. Typically 5-10% of contract value.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (9.3.1); state lien laws; Miller Act (federal)
Common Values: 5% standard, 10% on first 50% then 0%, graduated release, 0% (waived)
Procore Coverage: Medium — retainage tracked on invoices and commitments but retainage TERMS (percentage, release conditions) are not structured fields
Benchmarking Use: Retainage rates and release timing benchmark payment practices by market and owner type
Notes: Retainage rules vary by state law. Some states cap retainage at 5%. Financial detail (actual retainage amounts) lives in Financial Instruments domain.

Description: Pre-agreed daily or milestone-based financial penalty for late completion. Establishes the cost of delay without requiring the owner to prove actual damages. A critical risk term that drives schedule urgency.
Industry Standard: AIA A201 (4.3); AGC; state contract law
Common Values: $500-$50,000/day depending on project size and type. Milestone-based LDs for phased projects.
Procore Coverage: None — LD terms are in contract documents, not tracked in PM software. LD calculations happen in Excel or ERP.
Benchmarking Use: LD exposure by project type and contract value benchmarks schedule risk pricing
Notes: LDs create strong incentive alignment but are almost never tracked in construction PM systems. LD exposure is computed from contract terms × schedule variance.

Description: A budgeted amount included in the contract for work that cannot be fully defined at contract execution. Spent against actual costs as scope is clarified. Commonly used for concealed conditions, owner selections, or design development items.
Common Values: Owner selection allowance, concealed condition allowance, design development allowance, testing allowance
Procore Coverage: Medium — allowances can be tracked as budget line items but lack dedicated allowance tracking. Financial detail in Financial Instruments.
Benchmarking Use: Allowance burn rate and overrun frequency benchmark estimating accuracy and scope definition quality
Notes: Allowances are a financial planning tool detailed in Financial Instruments. Listed here for the commercial/contractual context — they define where scope risk is allocated.

Category: --- SURETY & INSURANCE: risk transfer instruments that protect parties from financial loss ---

Description: A surety bond submitted with a bid guaranteeing the bidder will enter into the contract at the bid price if awarded. Typically 5-10% of bid amount. Protects the owner against bidders who withdraw after bid opening.
Industry Standard: Miller Act (federal); Little Miller Acts (state); AGC
Common Values: 5% of bid amount (standard), 10% on public work
Source Systems: Surety companies; not tracked in PM software
Procore Coverage: None — bid bonds are paper/PDF documents, not tracked in construction PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Bid bond requirement frequency benchmarks procurement formality by owner type and project size
Notes: Required on virtually all public work. Private owners may waive bid bonds for prequalified contractors.

Description: Surety guarantee that the contractor will complete the work per contract terms. If the contractor defaults, the surety must complete the work or compensate the owner. Typically 100% of contract value.
Industry Standard: Miller Act; Little Miller Acts; AIA A312
Common Values: 100% of contract value (standard), partial bonds on private work
Source Systems: Surety companies; not tracked in PM software
Procore Coverage: None — bond data not in Procore. Some tracking via compliance documents.
Benchmarking Use: Bonding capacity and bond rate trends benchmark contractor financial health by trade and market
Notes: Performance bonds are required on all federal projects >$150K and most public projects. Private owners increasingly require them on large projects.

Description: Surety guarantee that the contractor will pay subcontractors, suppliers, and laborers. Protects the project from mechanic's liens filed by unpaid parties. Typically 100% of contract value.
Industry Standard: Miller Act; Little Miller Acts; AIA A312; state lien laws
Common Values: 100% of contract value (standard)
Source Systems: Surety companies; not tracked in PM software
Procore Coverage: None — bond data not in Procore
Benchmarking Use: Payment bond claims frequency benchmarks subcontractor payment practices and financial health
Notes: Payment bonds replace mechanic's lien rights on public projects (where liens can't attach to government property). On private work, subs have both lien rights and bond claims.

Description: Proof of insurance coverage — documents that the contractor carries required general liability, workers' comp, auto, umbrella, and builders risk coverage. Verified before work begins and tracked throughout the project.
Industry Standard: ACORD forms (25, 28); AIA A201 (11.1); ISO CGL
Common Values: GL ($1M/$2M), WC (statutory), auto ($1M), umbrella ($5-10M), builders risk, professional liability
Source Systems: Procore; myCOI; PINS; Exigis; BCS; Jones
Procore Coverage: High — company_vendor_insurances and project_vendor_insurances tables are ready with structured policy data
Benchmarking Use: Insurance compliance rates and common coverage gaps benchmark risk management maturity
Notes: Procore has structured insurance tracking with expiration dates, coverage amounts, and compliance status. One of the stronger procurement data sets in the platform.

Description: Insurance policy covering physical loss or damage to the building under construction. Typically carried by the owner or GC and covers all parties. Separate from the contractor's general liability.
Common Values: Project value-based premium, named perils vs. all-risk, deductible structures
Source Systems: Insurance carriers; not typically in PM software
Procore Coverage: None — builders risk is an insurance policy, not tracked in construction PM systems
Benchmarking Use: Builders risk claims frequency and severity benchmark construction damage risk by project type and geography
Notes: Builders risk is a project-level policy, not per-contractor. Claims (fire, water damage, theft, weather) are major project risk events but tracked outside PM software.

Description: Post-construction guarantee that work will be free from defects for a specified period. Standard is 1 year from substantial completion; extended warranties apply to specific systems (roofing: 20 years, HVAC: 5 years, waterproofing: 10 years).
Common Values: 1-year general, 2-year mechanical, 5-year HVAC, 10-year structural, 20-year roofing
Procore Coverage: Low — warranty tracking is minimal in Procore. Some use of action plans or custom tools for warranty items.
Benchmarking Use: Warranty claim rates by trade and building system benchmark construction quality and material durability
Notes: Warranty management is a major gap in construction PM systems — most tracking happens in facility management or spreadsheets after project closeout.

Description: The milestone when the project is sufficiently complete for the owner to occupy and use for its intended purpose. Triggers warranty start dates, retainage release, and final completion punchlist. A contractual — not just physical — milestone.
Common Values: Certificate of Substantial Completion (AIA G704), punch list issuance, owner occupancy
Source Systems: Procore; P6; MS Project; contract documents
Procore Coverage: Medium — trackable via project milestones or custom fields but no dedicated substantial completion entity in Procore
Benchmarking Use: Time from substantial completion to final completion benchmarks closeout efficiency
Notes: Substantial completion is the most important contractual milestone — it shifts risk, starts warranties, and triggers final payments. Often contested.

Description: The point at which ALL contract work is complete — all punch items resolved, all closeout documents delivered, all training completed. Triggers final retainage release and contract closeout. The true end of the project.
Common Values: Final payment application, consent of surety, all closeout docs delivered
Procore Coverage: Medium — trackable via project status and punch item completion but no dedicated final completion tracking
Benchmarking Use: Substantial-to-final completion duration benchmarks closeout discipline and team follow-through
Notes: The gap between substantial and final completion is often months. Long gaps signal poor closeout management and create retainage/lien exposure.

Category: --- COMPLIANCE DOCUMENTS: required documentation for regulatory and contractual compliance ---

Description: Documentation proving that workers on public projects are paid at or above the locally determined prevailing wage rate. Required on federal (Davis-Bacon) and most state public work. Submitted with each pay period.
Industry Standard: Davis-Bacon Act; state prevailing wage laws; DOL
Common Values: Certified payroll reports, WH-347 forms, apprenticeship ratios, fringe benefit statements
Procore Coverage: Medium — contract_compliance_documents table exists and is ready for import
Benchmarking Use: Certified payroll compliance rates benchmark labor law adherence by trade and geography
Notes: Prevailing wage enforcement is increasing. Non-compliance results in fines, contract termination, and debarment. Procore tracks compliance docs but not payroll detail.

Description: Documentation of participation by disadvantaged, minority, women-owned, veteran-owned, or small businesses. Required on most public projects and increasingly on private work. Tracked as percentage of contract value.
Industry Standard: FAR (federal); DOT DBE program; state and local programs
Common Values: DBE, MBE, WBE, SDVOSB, HUBZone, Section 3, local hire
Procore Coverage: Low — can be tracked via vendor custom fields or compliance documents but no dedicated diversity tracking
Benchmarking Use: DBE/MBE/WBE participation rates benchmark equity and inclusion performance by project type and market
Notes: Diversity requirements are growing in both public and private sectors. Most tracking is manual — spreadsheets and compliance portals. Major automation opportunity.

Description: The collection of documents required for project handover — as-builts, O&M manuals, warranties, training records, commissioning reports, and final lien waivers. The deliverable set that closes out the contract.
Common Values: As-built drawings, O&M manuals, warranty letters, test reports, training records, attic stock, spare parts
Procore Coverage: Medium — action plans and closeout punch lists can track deliverables, but no dedicated closeout tracking entity
Benchmarking Use: Closeout document completion rates and handover timelines benchmark project delivery quality
Notes: Closeout is universally hated in construction — it's administrative work after the exciting part is done. Completion tracking is the key metric.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from procurement and contract data ---

Description: Percentage of project scope (by value) that was competitively bid vs. sole-sourced or negotiated. Formula: SUM(competitively_bid_contract_value) / total_contract_value. Higher rate = more price discovery.
Common Values: 60-90% competitively bid on hard-bid projects, 40-70% on negotiated/CM projects
Procore Coverage: None — requires bid data not available in the data warehouse
Benchmarking Use: Bid coverage rates by delivery method and project type benchmark procurement rigor
Notes: Hard-bid projects approach 100% coverage. CM/GC and design-build projects have lower bid coverage because scopes are negotiated.

Description: Mean number of competing proposals received per bid package. Formula: AVG(bidders_per_package). More bidders = more price competition and market depth.
Common Values: 3-5 bidders typical, <3 signals tight market, >6 signals commodity scope
Procore Coverage: None — bid response data not in the data warehouse
Benchmarking Use: Bidder count by trade and market reveals subcontractor availability and competitive dynamics
Notes: Low bidder counts may indicate: market overheating, overly restrictive prequalification, unclear scope, or poor bid timing.

Description: Mean elapsed time from bid opening to contract execution. Formula: AVG(contract_date - bid_due_date). Shorter cycles get subs mobilized faster and reduce price escalation risk.
Common Values: 2-4 weeks (simple), 4-8 weeks (complex/negotiated), 8-12+ weeks (public/federal)
Procore Coverage: None — requires bid date tracking not in the data warehouse
Benchmarking Use: Award cycle time by procurement method and project type benchmarks organizational decision speed
Notes: Long award cycles create risk: subcontractors may withdraw bids, material prices escalate, and schedule slips. Track from bid due date to NTP.

Description: Percentage of active subcontractors with current, compliant insurance certificates. Formula: COUNT(compliant_vendors) / COUNT(active_vendors). Below 100% = uninsured work exposure.
Industry Standard: AIA A201; ACORD; owner risk management policies
Common Values: 95-100% target, <90% is a red flag
Procore Coverage: High — computable from company_vendor_insurances and project_vendor_insurances with expiration dates
Benchmarking Use: Insurance compliance rates benchmark risk management discipline across companies and projects
Notes: Procore has structured insurance data with expiration tracking. This is one of the most computable procurement metrics in the platform.

Description: Ratio of total change order value to original contract value. Formula: SUM(CO_value) / original_contract_value. Higher rate = more scope change, design issues, or unforeseen conditions.
Common Values: 5-10% typical, >15% signals problems, <3% may signal under-scoping
Procore Coverage: High — computable from commitments and commitment_change_orders tables
Benchmarking Use: Change order rates by contract type, trade, and project type are the most widely used procurement health metrics
Notes: This metric also appears in Financial Instruments (cost perspective). Here it's the contractual perspective — which contracts generate the most change.

Description: Percentage of subcontractors used on previous projects that are re-engaged on new projects. Formula: COUNT(repeat_subs) / COUNT(total_subs). Higher rate = stronger trade partner relationships.
Common Values: 40-60% typical for large GCs, >70% for relationship-focused builders
Procore Coverage: Medium — computable from project_vendors across projects but requires cross-project analysis
Benchmarking Use: Retention rates benchmark subcontractor relationship management — high retention correlates with fewer quality issues
Notes: Repeat subcontractors tend to have lower deficiency rates and fewer change orders. Cross-reference with quality and financial metrics.
